research_answer: The typical probability of jointly exporting two products at a given point in time is around 27% (with a standard deviation of 19%), but the probability of a country transitioning into a new product within 5 years — when it currently doesn't export it — averages only 8% (standard deviation of 12%). The transition rate is roughly one-third of the joint-exporting rate, reflecting the substantial difficulty of building new export capabilities. Given that the average 5-year transition rate is 8%, the 9% transition from raw cotton to processed cotton and 3% transition from raw sugar to confectionery fall at or below average — meaning that downstream processing is no easier to break into than a typical new product, despite having the raw material input at hand.
supporting_quotes: Furthermore, of the countries that exported raw cotton in 1995 but did not export processed cotton, only 9% were able to gain comparative advantage in processed cotton over the next 5 years.
Only 3% of raw sugar exporters who did not export confectionary products in 1995 were able to discover that sector by 2000

data_points.metric: Transition from raw cotton to processed cotton
Transition from raw sugar to confectionery
